questions.question: Why is keeping all your money in a savings account risky in the long term?
options: Banks might go out of business
Inflation can erode your purchasing power faster than savings interest grows
Savings accounts have too many fees
The government taxes savings more than investments
questions.explanation: If inflation (typically 2-3%/year) is higher than your savings interest rate (typically 0.5-2%), your money loses purchasing power over time. $10,000 today won't buy as much in 20 years if it only earns savings interest.

questions.question: Starting to invest at age 25 vs. 35 can more than double your final portfolio value, even with the same monthly contributions.
questions.explanation: Thanks to compound growth, those extra 10 years make a massive difference. $200/month at 7% from age 25-65 yields ~$525,000, while the same from 35-65 yields only ~$243,000. Time is an investor's greatest advantage.

questions.question: What is an index fund?
options: A fund that invests in only one company
A basket of many stocks that tracks a market index, providing instant diversification
A high-risk fund managed by expert traders
A savings account with variable interest
questions.explanation: An index fund holds many stocks that mirror a market index (like the S&P 500). It provides instant diversification, has very low fees, and is widely recommended for beginner investors.

questions.question: A common rule of thumb for stock allocation is (110 - your age). For a 30-year-old, this suggests approximately:
options: 30% in stocks
50% in stocks
80% in stocks
110% in stocks
questions.explanation: 110 - 30 = 80, suggesting about 80% in stocks and 20% in bonds. Younger investors can take more risk because they have decades to recover from market downturns.

questions.question: Diversification means putting all your money into the stock with the highest returns.
questions.explanation: Diversification is the opposite — it means spreading your money across many different investments to reduce risk. 'Don't put all your eggs in one basket' is the core principle.

questions.question: What should you have in place BEFORE you start investing?
options: A financial advisor on retainer
At least $10,000 in savings
An emergency fund and no high-interest debt
A degree in finance
questions.explanation: Before investing, you should have an emergency fund (3-6 months of expenses) and pay off high-interest debt (like credit cards). You don't need a lot of money or a finance degree to start.

questions.question: What does 'dollar-cost averaging' mean?
options: Only buying when prices are at their lowest
Investing a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price
Averaging the returns of multiple investments
Converting foreign currencies to dollars before investing
questions.explanation: Dollar-cost averaging means investing the same amount regularly (e.g., $200/month), regardless of market conditions. You buy more shares when prices are low and fewer when high, potentially lowering your average cost over time.

questions.question: Trying to predict the best time to buy and sell stocks (timing the market) is a reliable strategy for beginners.
questions.explanation: Market timing is extremely difficult even for professionals. Research shows that 'time IN the market' consistently beats 'timing the market.' Missing just the 10 best trading days over 20 years can cut your returns nearly in half.
